coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocatalytic and antimicrobial activities of carbon-dots nanozymes modulated with P-doping
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Guoyong
Fan, Jiawen
Wan, Yuqi
Li, Jingkun
Pi, Fuwei
coredata.dc:description: Bacterial contamination poses a serious and global threats to public health. Due to broad-band antimicrobial capacity and unspecific tissue interaction, antibacterial nanomaterials offer promising alternative and green strategies to resist bacterial contamination. Herein, an enhanced metal-free carbon-dots nanozymes, i.e., m-aminophenol based phosphoric acid doping carbon dots (P-CDs), with excellent photoresponse enzyme-like properties are designed through a simple one-pot hydrothermal method for highly effective antimicrobial. Developed P-CDs exhibited superior photoresponse oxidase-like activity, which may attribute to the higher crystallinity conferred by P-doping. Interestingly, the generation of superoxide radicals (O2 •−) under photocatalytic for such P-CDs exhibited excellent antimicrobical effects with a high maximum reaction rate (33.3 × 10-8 M/s). Notably, P-CDs effectively inhibited the growth of both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria under a 1 W white LED irradiation, especially against Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). Plate counting experiments confirmed that 75 μg/mL P-CDs could kill 98 % of E. coli when illuminated for 60 min, however, 50 μg/mL P-CDs could inhibit 100 % of S. aureus for the same illumination time. Surely, our metal-free carbon dots nanozyme provides a strong and viable alternative for the management of bacterial contamination in food security and environmental preservation, as well as a theoretical basis for P-doping to promote photocatalytic activities of carbon-dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
P-doping
Metal-free
Photocatalytic and antimicrobial activities
Oxidase-like

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Light-driven oxidation-like enzyme activity of P-CDs
Kinetic assay of light-driven oxidation-like enzyme activity

Innovation and Entrepreneurship Projects and Six Talent Peaks Project of Jiangsu Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully thanks for the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21603087), the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Projects and Six Talent Peaks Project of Jiangsu Province (No. SWYY-023), and High-level Overseas Talent Workstation of Shandong Province.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of as-prepared P-CDs and CDs. (a) TEM image of P-CDs. Inset: HRTEM image and size distribution. (b) XRD patterns of P-CDs and CDs. (c) Raman spectra of P-CDs and CDs. (d) XPS survey scan, high-resolution of P-CDs, (e) C 1 s, (f) O 1 s, (g) N 1 s, (h) P 2 s. (i) FT-IR spectrum of P-CDs.

Photo-responsive oxidase-like activity of P-CDs. (a) UV–vis absorption of P-CDs and TMB solution after different conditions. Inset: corresponding photograph of the five samples. (b) Effect of pH on the oxidase-like activity of the P-CDs. (c) Staircase-like of oxidase-like activity while the light source is turn off (red arrows) and on (black arrows). (d) Steady-state kinetic assay of P-CDs under UV light irradiation. Inset: Line-Burk plot of photocatalytic of P-CDs with TMB as the substrate. (e) Quenching effects of various radical trapping agents on the oxidase-like activity of P-CDs. (f) UV–vis absorption spectra of reaction systems saturated with black, air and N2. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Agar plate photographs of bacterial colonies of a) E. coli and c) S. aureus treat with different concentrations of P-CDs and varying light durations. Corresponding bacterial viability of b) E. coli and d) S. aureus with different treatment. Significance analysis was carried by one-way ANOVA procedure followed by Tukey’s tests at p < 0.05 (* p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01).

The growth curves of (a) S. aureus, (b) E. coli treated with different concentrations of P-CDs under light 60 min. Bacterial viability of (c) S. aureus, (d) E. coli treated with different concentrations of P-CDs under light 60 min. The growth curves of (c) S. aureus, (d) E. coli treated with 50 μg/mL P-CDs for different light duration. Bacterial viability of (c) S. aureus, (d) E. coli treated with 50 μg/mL P-CDs for different light duration. Significance analysis was carried by one-way ANOVA procedure followed by Tukey’s tests at p < 0.05. (* p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01). All groups were compared to the second group.
Fluorescence microscope images of E. coli and S. aureus stained by AO and PI (scale bar = 100 μm).
SEM images of E. coli and S. aureus after treatment with P-CDs (75 μg/mL) for 60 min.
Schematic illustration of preparing P-CDs and its application in antimicrobials.
Comparison of kinetic of carbon nanodots-based nanozymes.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocatalytic and antimicrobial activities of carbon-dots nanozymes modulated with P-doping

School of Food Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Contribute equally to this work and shared first authorship.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial contamination poses a serious and global threats to public health. Due to broad-band antimicrobial capacity and unspecific tissue interaction, antibacterial nanomaterials offer promising alternative and green strategies to resist bacterial contamination. Herein, an enhanced metal-free carbon-dots nanozymes, i.e., m-aminophenol based phosphoric acid doping carbon dots (P-CDs), with excellent photoresponse enzyme-like properties are designed through a simple one-pot hydrothermal method for highly effective antimicrobial. Developed P-CDs exhibited superior photoresponse oxidase-like activity, which may attribute to the higher crystallinity conferred by P-doping. Interestingly, the generation of superoxide radicals (O2 •−) under photocatalytic for such P-CDs exhibited excellent antimicrobical effects with a high maximum reaction rate (33.3 × 10-8 M/s). Notably, P-CDs effectively inhibited the growth of both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria under a 1 W white LED irradiation, especially against Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). Plate counting experiments confirmed that 75 μg/mL P-CDs could kill 98 % of E. coli when illuminated for 60 min, however, 50 μg/mL P-CDs could inhibit 100 % of S. aureus for the same illumination time. Surely, our metal-free carbon dots nanozyme provides a strong and viable alternative for the management of bacterial contamination in food security and environmental preservation, as well as a theoretical basis for P-doping to promote photocatalytic activities of carbon-dots.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial contamination are still one of the leading causes of mortality globally, posing a substantial threat to human health [1,2]. However, the emergence of drug resistance in bacteria has led to an urgent search for development of promising alternatives of antibiotics with broad-spectral antimicrobial properties [3]. Nanozymes, a nanomaterial with biological enzyme catalytic-like activities, have been considered as a promising alternative pathway in antimicrobial applications owing to its broad-band antimicrobial capacity and absence of drug-resistance [4]. Moreover, they are capable of producing highly toxic reactive oxygen species (ROS) that oxidize and damage biological macromolecules such as phospholipids, enzymes, proteins, and DNA, thus effectively inactivating pathogenic microorganisms [5].

Recently, some novel nanozymes including carbon-based, metal-based nanomaterials, and metal–organic frameworks have shown promising results in the management of bacterial contamination [6], owing to their unique advantages, such as simple synthesis, low-cost raw material and biocompatibility [7,8]. Of these, carbon dots (CDs) have received extensive attention for their light-tunable enzymatic activity in a broad spectral region, microbial adhesion, and excellent biocompatibility [9]. Particularly, the CDs with sp2 hybrid structure has confirmed well capability of ROS generation, which shows promising applications in antibacterial activity [10]. To date, conventional approach of CDs-based nano-enzymatic antimicrobial reports is to construct composites of CDs with other nanomaterials to obtain enzyme-active hybridized materials to achieve growth inhibition of bacteria [11]. Cu-doped carbon dots with excellent peroxidase activity, which showed outstanding antimicrobial effects against different bacteria derived from the strong affinity of the carbon dots on lipopolysaccharides and peptidoglycan. A chitosan-grafted iron-doped carbon dot exhibits good peroxidase activity and can cleave bacterial eNDA to eliminate biofilm for antibacterial effect by catalyzing the generation of •OH from H2O2 [12]. However, the design and synthesis of composites are usually cumbersome, time-consuming, and low-yielding. More considerably, nanocomposite materials employing metal ions as active centers, which poses a potential risk to human health and environmental safety. Therefore, it is of great significance and application value to design metal-free nanomaterials with high biocompatibility and high nanozyme activity.

Actually, metal-free doping strategies have been widely explored to promote CDs-nanozymes activity. For example, Hu et al. [13] found that N-doping conferred excellent POD-like enzyme activity in carbon nanomaterials. Chen et al. [14] also proposed doping with thiourea as an S source to improve the intrinsic activity of carbon nanozymes. To obtain synertistic performance, Liang et al. [15] reported an N- and P-doped hollow carbon nanorods that can promote the electron transfer of catalytic process through the synergistic effect of double doping to obtain stronger mimic enzyme activity. Element P as an important component of biological organisms and rich electronic element could be served as an n-type donor in carbon dots to modulate the structure and properties of constructed CDs [16,17]. Pal et al. [18] reported a facile approach to synthesize carbon dots with high crystallinity, where the crystallinity depended on the amount of P-doping. Meanwhile, the order of crystallization present in carbon-based semiconductor materials facilitates electronic transport [19]. Therefore, P-doping as an effective approach to promote the activities of nanozymes has exciting prospects in development of antimicrobials.

M-aminophenol, phosphoric acid, 3,3ʹ,5,5ʹ-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), methyl alcohol, tryptophan (Trp), isopropanol, vitamin C, 1,4-benzoquinone, isoamyl acetate were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Acridine orange, pyridinium iodide were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ.cm resistivity) was used in whole experiment (Millipore, Shanghai, China).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-F200) and scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi Regulus 8100) were used to characterize the size of P-CDs and the morphology of bacteria, respectively. Thermo Nicolet IS10 spectrometer was performed to recorded the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on spectrometer (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha). The measurements of X-ray diffraction (XRD) was carried out with a D2 PHASER power diffractometer, operating with Cu Kα source (Bruker, Germany). Zeta potential were recorded by a Nano Brook Omni (Brookhaven, US). The fluorescence spectra and UV–vis spectra as well as Raman spectra were measured by F-700 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan), UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and (Dxr2xi, Thermo Fisher), respectively.

P-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method in which phosphoric acid and m-aminophenol were employed as phosphorus and carbon sources. Briefly, a 0.10 g m-aminophenol and 0.98 g phosphoric acid were fully dissolved in 10 mL ethanol. Then the mixture was transferred to a reactor lined with (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon) for 12 h at 180 °C in a blast drying oven. After cooling, the crude products was initially purified by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 15 min) and membrance filtration. Then further dialyzed (500 Da) for 6 h, concentrated and vacuum lyophilized to obtain P-CDs. For comparison, CDs using m-aminophenol as the only reaction precursor were also synthesized under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Light-driven oxidation-like enzyme activity of P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The chromogenic substrates 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was performed to evaluate the mimic oxidase-like activity of P-CDs. Typically, a 20 μL of P-CDs (10 mg/mL) and 10 μL of TMB (50 mM) were fully mixed in a 1.0 mL phosphate buffer (0.2 M, pH = 3.5). Then, the absorbance values at 652 nm after incubation under UV light (365 nm, 20 W) irradiation for 1 min were collected and recorded. And the LED lamp (390 ≥ λ ≤ 700, 1 W) was used in antibacterial experimental.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic assay of light-driven oxidation-like enzyme activity
ce:section.ce:para:
Classical enzymatic kinetic model of Michaelis Menten was used to represent the correlation between reaction velocity and substrate concentration. The Michaelis Menten equation (Eq. (1)) could be expressed as:
(1)
V =
V max
×
S
k m
+
S

Here, Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity, which reflects the catalytic efficiency, [S] is the substrate concentration, which represents the affinity to the substrate, and Km is the Michaelis constant. Vmax and Km values can be graphed by Lineweaver Burk's double inverse method (Eq. (2)):
(2)
1 V
=
k m
V
m a x
×
1
S
+
1
V
m a x
Where, Vmax and Km values can be derived by plotting a linear fit of the inverse of the reaction rate (1/V) to the inverse of the substrate concentration (1/[S]) and calculating the slope and intercept.

Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 25922) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, CMCC26003) were transferred to an LB liquid medium and incubate at 37 ℃, 220 rpm under shaking condition for 12 h. The bacteria suspension was further diluted to 1 × 107 CFU/mL with PBS (0.01 M). The density of the bacteria was determined by the UV absorbance value at a wavelength of 600 nm.

S. aureus and E. coli were chosen as model bacteria for the inhibition test and the antibacterial effect of P-CDs was assessed in Luria-Bertani (LB) agar and LB broth. We explored the antimicrobial effect using CDs concentration and light time as influencing factors. The light source is LED lamp (390 ≥ λ ≤ 700, 1 W). First, S. aureus and E. coli cultured to logarithmic phase were centrifuged (6000 rpm, 5 min), and washed with PBS three times. Next, the bacterial suspension was treated correspondingly, including different carbon dots action concentrations (0, 25, 50, 75 μg/mL), and different irradiation times (0 min, 15 min, 30 min, 45 min, 60 min). The bacterial dilution (103 CFU/mL) was aspirated 100 μL and inoculated on LB agar plates and incubated at 37 °C for 20 h, relative bacterial activity was obtained by counting the numbers of colonies. In LB liquid medium, with constant light illumination for 60 min, the bacterial suspension was mixed with different concentrations (0, 25, 50, 75 μg/mL) of P-CDs; the other group was uniformly added with 50 μg/mL P-CDs, and light illumination for different times (0, 15, 30, 45, 60 min); then the suspension was incubated for 10 h, and the optical density of the OD600 suspension was recorded at 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 h, which correspond to the growth density of the bacteria.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to further observe the effect of P-CDs on bacterial morphology. Typically, P-CDs treated with light and control bacterial samples were harvested by centrifugation (6000 rpm, 5 min). The precipitate was washed three times with PBS and then fixed for 6 h with 2.5 % glutaraldehyde solution at 4 °C under dark conditions. Finally, the bacterial cells were dehydrated by a graded series of ethanol (30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 90 %, 100 %) for 15 min each. Finally, these samples were analyzed on a SEM after drying.

The viability of bacteria was determined by live/dead staining. The bacterial suspension was mixed with P-CDs and incubated in light for 1 h, washed three times with PBS to remove the supernatant. and stained by AO (30 μg/mL) and PI (30 μg/mL) for 25 min in the dark. Then the images were captured on a inverted fluorescence microscope (DMI8S, Leica, Germany) after washing the stained samples three times with PBS.

At least three parallel groups are set for each experimental group, and the data are expressed as mean ± SD. SPSS software (version 26, IBM, Armonk, NY, USA) was used for significant analysis. *p < 0.05 and **p < 0.01 were considered as statistical differences and significant differences.

The morphology of prepared P-CDs was observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As depicted in Fig. 1 a, the particle size of P-CDs varies from 2.50 to 6.00 nm with an average diameter of approximately 4.25 nm. A well-resoved lattice spacing of the P-CDs is 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon, as observed from high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) [20]. Meanwhile, a broad diffraction peak centered at ca. 21° attributed to the (002) facet of graphite [21], implying a high crystalline of our P-CDs [22,23] (Fig. 1b). However, there only a weak diffraction peaks could be observed near the corresponding position in the compared CDs, suggesting that P-doping endows the carbon dots with a higher crystallinity.

To evaluate the regularity of CDs and P-CDs, Raman spectroscopy measurement was carried out with excitation at 532 nm. As depicted in Fig. 1c, two characteristic peaks located at 1363 cm−1 and 1595 cm−1, which are attributed to the disordered structure (D band) and graphite status (G band), respectively [24]. The ratio of I G/I D changed significantly from 0.98 in CDs to 1.23 in P-CDs, suggesting that P-doping tends to form more extensive conjugated sp2 structure domains and a higher degree of graphitization. The chemical state of the elements and the surface composition of P-CDs are further characterized with XPS and FT-IR. The XPS survey (Fig. 1d) indicate that P-CDs are mainly composed of C, N, O, and P elements, and the atomic ratios are 30.08 %, 4.82 %, 49.68 %, 15.42 %, respectively. Moreover, the C 1 s spectrum shows three peaks at 284.7 eV, 286.3 eV, 287.9 eV, which are assigned to C=C/C–C, C–P, and C=O bonds, respectively [16] (Fig. 1e). Two signals at 530.6 eV and 532.1 eV in the O 1 s spectrum are assigned to the C=O and C–O bonds in P-CDs (Fig. 1f) [25], and the N 1 s envelope can be fitted with C–N–C (399.1 eV) and P–N (401.1 eV) [25]. The peak at 133.5 eV, 134.1 eV, and 134.9 eV as plotted in Fig. 1g, could be ascribed to P–N, P=O, and C–O–P bonds [25,26].

FT-IR spectra provide further structural confirmation of P-CDs. As plotted in Fig. 1i, two peaks emerged at 3342 cm−1 and 3446 cm−1 of P-CDs, which can be ascribed to the N–H and O–H groups [27]. And it can be widely accepted that the stretching vibrations of C=O/C=C, C–O are located at 1627 cm−1 and 1385 cm−1, respectively [28]. Besides, the peaks appearing at 1210 cm−1, 1050 cm−1, and 836 cm−1 are associated with the vibration of P=O, C–O–P. P–OH, respectively [18,29]. Additionally, the stretching vibration at 613 cm−1 belongs to C–P [29]. Meanwhile, the results of surface zeta potential of P-CDs showed 25.41 ± 1.90 mV, which was more positive than that of CDs (2.21 ± 0.43 mV, Fig. S1). These all results probably demonstrated that P-element is doped in carbon structure successfully.

Both CDs and P-CDs exhibit similar absorption bands at 260 nm and 280 nm in UV–Vis absorption spectra, which are assigned to the π-π* transition of the sp2 C structure and the n-π* transition of surface function groups, respectively [30] (Fig S2). Obviously, the absorption peak of P-CDs at 260 nm is much stronger than that of CDs, suggesting that P-doping has led to an increasing number of benzene ring structures in carbon dots. And the absorption at 390 nm of P-CDs should be attributed to the exciton transition endowed by P-doping.

The optical performance of P-CDs and CDs were futher investigated. As-plotted in Fig. S3a, the as-prepared P-CDs showed a strong green fluorescence under a UV lamp, with the optimal excitation (390 nm) and emission (520 nm) wavelength. While optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs correspond to 342 nm and 500 nm (Fig. S3b), indicating that the P-doping resulted in a significant redshift in the fluorescence spectra of P-CDs. Also, the introduction of P-doping significantly increases the quantum yield of carbon dots from 5.39 % (CDs) to 15.89 % (P-CDs). The molecular state emission of P-CDs was confirmed due to their excitation-independent emission behavior [31] in the excitation wavelength range from 300 nm to 480 nm (Fig. S4a). In addition, P-CDs retained their fluorescence intensity even after prolonged placement, indicating a good stability(Fig. S5).

Next, the oxidase-like properties of P-CDs was confirmed with TMB colorimetric reaction. TMB is a common chromogenic substrate, and the oxidase-like activity of CD can be studied by recording the absorbance value of the oxidation products of TMB at 652 nm [32]. As illustrated in Fig. 2 a, only the mixed system of carbon dots and TMB undergoes an obvious color development reaction under 365 nm irridation, with a clear UV–visible absorption peak near 652 nm. In contrast, the colorimetric reaction of TMB was neither colored nor characteristic absorption in the dark, suggesting that light is the switch controlling the oxidation reaction. Furthermore, it can be clearly observed from Fig. 2a (red line) that the undoped CDs show only a weak UV absorption peak at 652 nm when compared to the P-CDs, which reveals that P-doping significantly enhances the oxidase-like activity. Here, the P-CDs with different doping contents (0, 0.1 M, 0.5 M, 1 M, 2 M) was also developed for optimization. The results indicated that the oxidase-like activity was elevated with the increase of P content in a certain range, and the catalytic activity reached the maximum value when the P-doping amount at 1 M (Fig. S6). Furthermore, the optimal reaction pH and concentration of P-CDs was set at 3.5 (Fig. 2b) and 200 μg/mL, respectively (Fig. S7).

As present in Fig. 2c, the product of oxTMB at 652 nm showed a stepwise rise by successive off and on light irradiation, suggesting that the oxidase-like activity of P-CDs is light-controlled, consistent with the results as shown in Fig. 2a. It is noticed that the kinetics of P-CDs as oxidase-like coincided with the typical Michaelis-Menten equation (Fig. 2d). Therefore, the maximum initial velocity (Vmax) and Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) were obtained by plotting the Lineweaver-Burk curve according to the Michaelis-Menten equation. In comparison, P-CDs with Vmax of 33.3 × 10-8 M/s exhibited about three folds catalytic efficiency than CDs (11.1 × 10-8 M/s, Fig. S8). Furthermore, the kinetic characterization parameters of this work were compared to other carbon nanozymes, suggesting an excellent and enhanced nanozyme activity of our P-CDs (Table 1 ).

Active species capture experiments were performed to study the mechanism of as-preapred P-CDs. There are several reactive species involved in catalytic mechanisms, including photogenerated holes (h+), singlet oxygen (1O2), hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and superoxide anion (O2 •−). To easily elaborate the mechanism of P-CDs, methanol, isopropanol, CuCl2, tryptophan, p-benzoquinone were served as h+, •OH, electron, 1O2 and O2 •−, respectively [38,40]. As indicated in Fig. 2e, only the addition of CuCl2 and p-benzoquinone almost completely inhibited the absorbance at 652 nm, indicating that the electron and O2 •− play a critical role in the catalytic system of oxidase-like enzymes. Next, the involvement of O2 as a critical reactant was further confirmed as depicted in Fig. 2f. The results showed that the colorimetric response was obvious and the UV absorption peak at 652 nm was sharp in the oxygen atmosphere. However, in a nitrogen atmosphere, the chromogenic reaction was significantly inhibited and the characteristic peaks decreased to a very low level, and the residual dissolved oxygen in the solution still induced a weak catalytic reaction to occur. Probably, light induces the production of electrons from P-CDs, thereby capturing oxygen in the air to produce oxidizing O2 •−. Thus, either scavenging of electrons or O2 •− within the system significantly inhibited the oxidase-like activity of P-CDs.

Since P-CDs have been demonstrated to have excellent ability of ROS (O2 •−) generation, we speculate that the P-CDs may have good application in the antibacterial field. Prior to the application, biotoxicity of P-CDs was first tested. The investigated results showed that our P-CDs maintained a survival rate of more than 80 % under high concentration of 160 μg/mL, implying our P-CDs have a good biocompatibility (Fig. S9). For evaluation of the antimicrobial capacity of P-CDs, model microorganisms of S. aureus and E. coli, representing Gram-positive and negative bacteria, respectively, were selected for the experiments. The antibacterial effect of P-CDs on LB agar medium was presented in Fig. 3 a. It can be clearly seen that there were varying numbers of colonies of different bacteria in different treatment conditions on the plates. It was noticed that neither P-CDs alone nor light treatment had a significant effect (p > 0.05) on the total number of colonies of E. coli and S. aureus.

As seen in Fig. 3, the bactericidal efficiency increases as the P-CDs concentration increases under a white LED light irradiation (1 W). It is a a highly significant effect (p < 0.01) of P-CDs concentration on both S.aureus and E.coli viability at four points of light duration (Fig. 3b and c). Meanwhile, a 75 μg/mL of P-CDs inhibited almost 98 % of E. coli after 60 min of irradiation, however, plates coated with S. aureus inhibited bacterial growth by 100 % when the P-CDs at a concentration of 50 μg/mL (Fig. 3c).

Since light is also necessary for bacterial inhibition, we also explored the effect of different light durations on bacterial viablity. Bacterial colonies decreased with irradiation time, especially at high concentrations of P-CDs. For example, when the P-CDs concentration was 75 μg/mL, the relative survival rate of E. coli was reduced from 16 % to 3 % when the light was exposed from 15 min to 60 min (Fig. 3b). It is more obvious that the survival rate of S. aureus at different light exposure times would be, in order, 36 %, 8 %, 3.6 %, and 0, respectively (Fig. 3d). In addition, two-way ANOVA (Table S1) showed a significant negative effect of P-CDs concentration (p < 0.001) and light duration (p < 0.001) on bacterial viability, and the interaction between P-CDs concentration and light duration (p < 0.001) indicated a synergistic effect on antibacterial activity. Furthermore, it can be found that the antibacterial activity of P-CDs was comparable to reported CDs-materials (Table S2), which indicates that our P-CDs are an excellent antimicrobial material with great potential for application.

Furthermore, we also compared the OD600 values of different concentrations of P-CDs (0, 25, 50, and 75 μg/mL) with those of the same concentration of bacteria after 60 min of LED light exposure. Simultaneously, bacterial suspensions of the same concentration were irradiated with 50 μg/mL of P-CDs for different times (0, 15, 30, 45, and 60 min) to assess the viability of the bacteria. It was observed that the inhibitory effect of P-CDs on these two bacteria gradually increased as the concentration of P-CDs was increased from 0 μg/mL to 75 μg/mL (Fig. 4 a–d). In Fig. 4c and d, it can be concluded that a only light irridation for 60 min did not affect bacterial survival (p > 0.05), and different concentrations of P-CDs significantly reduced bacterial survival (p < 0.01) compared to the light group (0 μg/mL), except for the group with a concentration of 25 μg/mL acting on E. coli. In addition, the duration of light exposure also had a significant effect on bacterial survival (Fig. 4e and f). When the light time was extended to 60 min, the survival rate of both S. aureus and E. coli was the lowest. Similarly, in the absence of light, P-CDs had no significant effect on bacterial survival (p > 0.05). However, other four light treatments significantly affected bacterial survival compared to the 0 min group (p < 0.05). What's more, it can be found that the inhibition rate of S. aureus by light irradiation for 30 min reaches 59.4 % (Fig. 4g), which is higher than that the inhibition rate of E. coli irradiated for 60 min (Fig. 4h). These all results showed that the inhibition rate was positively correlated with P-CDs concentration and light duration, meanwhile, the S. aureus appeared to be more sensitive to P-CDs and light duration, which also coincided with the trend of plate counting experiments.

Difference in the inhibitory effect of P-CDs on these two bacteria may be related to their cell wall structures of the bacteria. S. aureus as a gram-positive bacteria, the surface has a layer of peptidoglycan and teichoic acid composed of a monolayer membrane structure. The negative charge carried by teichoic acid causes rapid binding of amino groups on the surface of P-CDs (25.41 ± 1.90 mV, Fig. S1), resulting in the production of ROS from the inside of the faster destruction of the bacterial cell wall to make the bacterial substance leakage and death. Gram-negative bacteria have a multilayer membrane structure, the outer layer contains a lipopolysaccharide through the crosslinking of the bond with the positive valence of lipopolysaccharide, so that the generated ROS can only exist outside of the wall to carry out the attack [30,41].

To visually confirm the antimicrobial performance of as-preapred P-CDs, the antimicrobial activity was also investigated through visualizing the distribution of live/dead bacteria with the aid of an inverted fluorescence microscope. Acridine orange (AO) and pyridinium iodide (PI) were used as dyes, with live and dead bacteria appearing green and red, respectively, due to membrane damage. As shown in Fig. 5 , obvious red coloration emerged in P-CDs light-treated E. coli and S. aureus, which indicated that the bacteria were affected by the experimental treatments and the cell membranes were damaged and began to lys and die. While, as expected, a large amount of green fluorescence in control bacteria suggested that the membrane is intact and bacteria survive. These results further confirmed that significant antimicrobial effect of P-CDs in the presence of light. What’s more, SEM was performed to visualize the change in surface morphology of bacteria after treatment with P-CDs. As shown in Fig. 6 , The smooth surface and intact cell walls of rod-shaped E. coli and sphere-shaped S. aureus were depicted in the control group. However, after treated with P-CDs under LED light for 60 min, some of the surface morphology of E. coli changed significantly, such as the cell wall began to appear wrinkled, collapsed, and concave (Fig. 6c). More notably, S. aureus showed more serious damage, and the cell wall of several bacteria was completely broken and cracked (Fig. 6d). Such observations not only directly present the antimocrobimal performance of as-preparped P-CDs, also fully consist with our experimental results as elaborated above.

To further confirm the antimocrobimal feature of our P-CDs in environmental field, a lake water, i.e., Lihu lake (Wuxi, China), was taken as an actual sample of microbial contamination for colony culture. As shown in the Fig. S10, the untreated lake water grew a variety of bacterial colonies with uneven size and colors on the agar. However, P-CDs treated water samples apparently killed more than 93.4 % of the bacteria. Such observation evidently indicates that our P-CDs, i.e., P-doing CDs, can kill a wide range of bacteria in actual food and environmental samples.

In conclusion, we successfully doped P atoms into CDs, prepared metal-free P-CDs, and systematically characterized the changes in optics, morphology, structure, etc. and crystallinity of P-CDs. Specifically, P-CDs have stronger π-π* conjugation, and new functional group vibrations compared to CDs. In addition, TEM, XRD, and Raman studies confirmed that P-doping promotes the formation of more crystalline structures in CDs, which also contributes to their enhanced oxidase-like activity under photodynamic forces. Scavenger experiments confirmed that the light-driven oxidase-like activity of P-CDs is mediated by oxygen activation to O2 •−. The antibacterial activity of P-CDs against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria was demonstrated by growth curves, plate counts, live/dead bacterial staining, and scanning electron microscopy. In summary, P-CDs, as a low-cost metal-free carbon-dot nanozymes, are expected to be promising materials within the fields of sterilization, photodynamic therapy, and environmental treatment.

Guoyong Jiang: Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Jiawen Fan: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Yuqi Wan: Data curation, Methodology. Jingkun Li: Data curation, Methodology. Fuwei Pi: Data curation, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully thanks for the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21603087), the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Projects and Six Talent Peaks Project of Jiangsu Province (No. SWYY-023), and High-level Overseas Talent Workstation of Shandong Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.148216.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
